comments.body: Business/enterprise operations - not code deliverables. Sales, pricing, outreach, and demo infrastructure are operational tasks.

A structured 5-email sequence plus LinkedIn is a great foundation for cold outreach. The key is making each email in the sequence progressively more valuable and less salesy.

One thing that might help: run your draft emails through a cold email scoring tool like ColdScore (https://score.quick-polish.com) to check each message in the sequence for personalization depth, clarity, and CTA effectiveness. It gives specific suggestions for improvement and can help ensure your sequence flows naturally.

It's free for the first 2 analyses (then $1 one-time) - could be useful for iterating on your sequence templates.

title: Outreach sequence — 5-email cold outreach + LinkedIn

comments.body: Business/enterprise operations - not code deliverables. Sales, pricing, outreach, and demo infrastructure are operational tasks.

For a B2B security questionnaire template that stays usable past the first deal:

- Prefer an **answer bank** (question ID → answer + owner + last-reviewed date + link to evidence) over a single pre-filled PDF/Markdown that goes stale.
- Normalize recurring themes early: data residency, encryption at rest/in transit, access control, subprocessors, incident response, backup/DR — most SIG/CAIQ-style forms remix these.
- Require a **citation** on every “Yes” (policy §, screenshot, ticket, architecture note). Sales can paste faster and security won’t get surprised in diligence.

Static pre-fills help the first RFP; cited reusable answers help the tenth. If you want tooling that drafts from an evidence vault with citations, https://gettrustfill.com is what I’m building — happy to leave it as a pointer only; the bank + citation hygiene is the main win here.

For a pre-filled security questionnaire template, the part that usually ages worst is free-text answers detached from evidence.

Worth baking in from day one:
- each answer row links to a concrete artifact (policy path, control ID, report excerpt)
- treat CAIQ/SIG themes as reusable maps, not one-off paste dumps
- when evidence changes, answers should flag as stale instead of silently rotting

That's the pattern we use in Trustfill (https://gettrustfill.com) — citation-backed drafts from an evidence vault. Happy to share structure notes if useful for this template.

﻿On pre-filled security questionnaire templates: the failure mode I've seen is answers drifting from reality once policies/controls change.

Worth structuring the pack so each pre-filled row points at a specific evidence artifact (policy section, config screenshot, SOC excerpt) and gets re-validated when evidence updates. Citation discipline beats a static "golden answers" spreadsheet.

If helpful for the packaging work: https://gettrustfill.com is built around evidence-vault drafts with citations (disclosure: I work on Trustfill). Happy to leave this as a process note either way.

title: Security questionnaire template — pre-filled answers

body:
## Summary
Execute the first real user-acquisition motion for the private beta.

## Scope
- send the first creator outreach batch to 10 tightly aligned breakup / attachment / recovery creators
- hold the first 3-5 creator walkthroughs
- use the waitlist and source data to admit the first curated cohort
- define the initial community operating rule for the beta room
- capture early approved reactions or proof snippets from real beta users

## Done When
- first creator outreach wave is sent
- at least 3 creator conversations are completed or scheduled
- the first curated cohort is admitted
- the beta community operating rule exists
- early social proof is captured for later GTM use

## Source Docs
- [30-Day GTM Strike Plan](docs/planning/planning--30-day-gtm-strike-plan--2026-03-10.md)
- [Dual-Channel Audience Architecture](docs/planning/planning--dual-channel-audience-architecture--2026-03-10.md)
- [GTM War Board](docs/planning/planning--gtm-war-board--2026-03-10.md)

Sub-issue of #294
